mainEntity.name: How does drug lab deodorization affect HVAC systems in older San Fernando Valley buildings?
mainEntity.text: How does drug lab deodorization affect HVAC systems in older San Fernando Valley buildings?

acceptedAnswer.text: In older buildings in San Fernando, Encino, or Tarzana, HVAC systems can act as both a distribution pathway and a reservoir for drug lab odors. During deodorization, restoration technicians typically begin with a full assessment of the system: return and supply vents, ductwork, blower compartments, and coils are checked for residue, staining, or unusual smells. If contamination is suspected, they may temporarily shut down the system and establish negative air containment to avoid spreading particles while work is performed. Duct cleaning using HEPA‑filtered equipment, replacement of all filters with higher‑efficiency options, and cleaning of coils and interior surfaces are common steps. In heavily impacted systems, sections of duct or old, fiber‑lined components might need removal. Technicians may also seal duct leaks, clean or replace grilles, and ensure that condensate pans and drain lines are free of residues that could contribute to odors. After deodorization, many companies recommend a period of enhanced filtration and monitoring to ensure odors do not reappear when the system runs. Property owners should ask whether the cleanup plan includes HVAC evaluation, what standards are followed for duct cleaning, and whether any components will need replacement due to contamination.
acceptedAnswer.description: Drug lab deodorization often requires inspecting and sometimes cleaning or removing parts of older HVAC systems. Contaminants and odors can lodge in ductwork, coils, and returns, so technicians may recommend duct cleaning, filter upgrades, sealing leaks, or replacing heavily impacted components to prevent odors from returning.
